explanation_md: `"cat"` has three letters plus a null terminator, so `sizeof(word)` is 4. `word[0]` is the first character `'c'`.

prompt_md: You declare `char buf[5]{ "Hello" };` in C++. What happens?

options.a: The array stores `H e l l o` with no null terminator, which is valid.
options.b: This is a compile error or truncates the string because `"Hello"` requires 6 bytes (5 letters + null terminator) but the array has only 5 slots.
options.c: The array is automatically resized to 6 to fit the null terminator.
options.d: The null terminator is placed at `buf[4]` by overwriting the last letter.

explanation_md: `"Hello"` requires 6 `char` slots: `H`, `e`, `l`, `l`, `o`, `\0`. A `char[5]` only has 5. In C++, this is ill-formed — the compiler will reject it or warn about the truncated initializer. Always allocate at least `length + 1` for the null terminator.
The loop counts characters until the null terminator. `"abc"` has three characters (`a`, `b`, `c`) followed by `\0`. The loop increments `len` 3 times before `*p` equals `\0`.

options.a: C-style strings cannot be stored in arrays.
options.b: `a` and `b` decay to pointers; `a == b` compares addresses (always false), not string content.
options.c: The `else` branch is unreachable because the strings are identical.
options.d: `a == b` compares the first characters only, not the whole string.

explanation_md: `a` and `b` are two separate arrays in different memory locations. When used in the `==` expression, they decay to `char*` pointers to those locations. The pointers are different, so the comparison is always false — it never compares the actual characters. Use `std::strcmp(a, b) == 0` or convert to `std::string` to compare content.

options.b: `msg` is not null-terminated; there is no `\0` after `msg[1]`, so `std::cout` reads past `i` until a random zero byte.
options.c: `msg[0]` and `msg[1]` cannot be assigned individually; the array must be initialized with a string literal.
options.d: The array size 10 is too large for a 2-character string.

explanation_md: `msg` is not zero-initialized, so the bytes after `msg[1]` contain garbage. `std::cout` reads characters until it finds a `\0`, so it prints `Hi` followed by whatever undefined bytes come next. The fix is either `char msg[10]{}` (zero-initialize all bytes) or explicitly write `msg[2] = '\0'`.
